title: The Relationship Between Presence and Financial Abundance
body:
The Uncomfortable Truth About Presence and Your Money

Truth is, you're probably broke or feeling squeezed, not because you lack a budget or a savings plan, but because you lack presence. Ouch. I know. It stings. But it's true. You've been told to hustle, to optimize, to strategize. You've downloaded apps, read books, listened to podcasts. You've probably even tried to "manifest" money with varying degrees of success, mostly none. And yet, here you are, still wrestling with the same financial anxieties, the same scarcity mindset, the same nagging feeling that there's never enough. Why? Because you're missing the most fundamental ingredient for abundance: being here now. Not yesterday's mistakes, not tomorrow's fears. Just this moment. Right here. With your money.

The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's not just about numbers on a screen. It's about your nervous system. It's about your childhood programming. It's about the collective trauma of scarcity that's been passed down through generations. It's about how you feel in your body when you think about money. It's about the stories you tell yourself, consciously and unconsciously, about what you deserve, what's possible, and who you are in relation to wealth. You can't outsmart these deeply ingrained patterns with a new financial product or a clever investment strategy. You have to meet them where they live: in your present moment awareness. Know what I mean?

The Illusion of Control and the Tyranny of the Future

You think you're in control when you're meticulously planning your retirement, or agonizing over every dollar spent. But often, that's just a sophisticated form of anxiety. You're living in the future, projecting all your hopes and fears onto an uncertain tomorrow. You're trying to control what is inherently uncontrollable. And in doing so, you're completely missing the richness, the potential, and the actual resources available to you right now. You're so busy worrying about what might happen that you can't even see what is happening. This isn't presence. This is a mental prison. And it keeps you perpetually feeling behind, no matter how much you earn or save.

Think about it. When you're constantly projecting into the future, you're operating from a place of "not enough." Not enough time, not enough money, not enough security. This scarcity mindset isn't just a thought; it's a physiological state. Your body tenses. Your breath shortens. Your mind races. This isn't a state conducive to creativity, wise decision-making, or attracting abundance. It's a state of contraction. And money, like everything else, responds to expansion, not contraction. You feel that?

Lynne Twist, in her powerful book The Soul of Money (paid link), talks about sufficiency. Not about having "enough" in a numerical sense, but about the experience of enough. It's a feeling, a state of mind, an internal knowing that you are, in this moment, complete. This isn't about ignoring your bills or being irresponsible. It's about approaching your financial reality from a place of internal resourcefulness, rather than internal deficit. It's a subtle but real shift. When you're present, you can actually assess what you have, what you need, and what you can create, without the overwhelming static of future-based fear.

Your Nervous System and Your Net Worth

Let's get real about your nervous system. Your financial habits aren't just logical choices; they're deeply wired responses. When you're stressed about money, your body goes into fight, flight, or freeze. This ancient survival mechanism isn't designed for complex financial decisions. It's designed to help you escape a tiger. So, when you're trying to make a budget while your amygdala is screaming "DANGER!", you're setting yourself up for failure. You'll either avoid the problem (freeze), spend impulsively (flight), or get aggressive with your spending/saving (fight). None of these are sustainable paths to financial well-being.

Presence is the antidote. When you bring presence to your financial situation, you're essentially telling your nervous system, "It's okay. We're safe right now. We can look at this." You're creating a space for calm, for clarity, for conscious choice. This doesn't mean the problems disappear. It means you can approach them from a place of internal regulation, rather than reactive chaos. Barbara Huson, who's done incredible work on women and money, often speaks about the emotional labor involved in financial healing. It's not just about learning how to invest; it's about learning how to be with your feelings about money, how to process the shame, the fear, the guilt, the anger. And you can't process those feelings if you're constantly running from them, or trying to intellectualize them away.

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It reflects back to you your deepest fears, your unhealed wounds, your true beliefs about yourself and the world. When you avoid looking at your bank statements, when you procrastinate on taxes, when you spend compulsively to soothe yourself, you're not just avoiding money. You're avoiding yourself. You're avoiding the discomfort, the vulnerability, the truth of your current situation. Presence invites you to stop running. It invites you to turn towards it, with curiosity and compassion.

The Practice of Present Moment Awareness with Money

So, how do you actually do this? It's not some magic trick. It's a practice. Like meditation, like yoga, like learning a new skill. It requires commitment and consistency. It's about bringing your attention, intentionally and non-judgmentally, to your financial reality, right here, right now.

1. The "Money Check-in" Ritual: Don't just glance at your bank account. Sit down. Set a timer for 5 minutes. Open your banking app or spreadsheet. Before you even look at the numbers, take three deep breaths. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice any tension in your body. Now, look at the numbers. Don't judge them. Just observe. What sensations arise in your body? What thoughts come up? Don't try to change them or fix them. Just notice. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. This isn't about making decisions. It's about building your capacity to be present with your financial reality, whatever it is. Do this daily, or at least a few times a week.

2. Mindful Spending: Before you buy anything - anything at all - pause. Just for a second. Ask yourself: "Am I buying this from a place of presence, or a place of avoidance/fear/impulse?" Is this aligned with my values? Is this a conscious choice, or am I on autopilot? This isn't about deprivation. It's about intention. Sometimes the answer will be "yes, I genuinely want this." Other times, you'll realize you're just trying to fill a void, or keep up with someone else, or distract yourself from something uncomfortable. That pause creates space for a different choice.

3. Acknowledging Your Resources (Beyond the Bank Account): Presence isn't just about what's in your bank account. It's about recognizing all the resources you have right now. Your skills, your knowledge, your network, your creativity, your health, your time, your energy. Often, we get so fixated on the number in our account that we become blind to the wealth of other resources at our disposal. Make a list. Seriously. Write down every resource you possess that isn't cash. You'll be surprised. This shifts your internal narrative from "I don't have enough" to "I have resources."

George Kinder, the founder of the Life Planning movement, emphasizes that money is a tool to live your most authentic life. But you can't even know what your most authentic life looks like if you're not present enough to feel into it, to clarify your deepest values, and to understand what truly matters to you. Without presence, money just becomes another source of stress, another thing to chase, another way to measure your perceived failures or successes against some arbitrary external standard. Sound familiar?

The Trap of Future-Pacing and Spiritual Bypassing

You've seen it. The "manifestation gurus" telling you to "act as if" you already have millions. The "positive thinking" proponents urging you to ignore your current financial struggles and just visualize abundance. While intention and visualization have their place, they can become a form of spiritual bypassing if they're used to avoid the uncomfortable truth of your present reality. You can't manifest abundance from a place of denial. You can't out-think your deeply embedded money wounds. You have to be present with what is, acknowledge it, and then, from that grounded place, you can consciously choose to create something different.

Sam Harris, the neuroscientist and philosopher, often speaks about the illusion of the self and the importance of being present to the raw data of consciousness. This applies directly to your money. Your thoughts about money, your feelings about money - they're just data. They're not "you." When you get caught in the story of "I'm bad with money" or "I'll never have enough," you're identifying with the data. Presence allows you to observe these thoughts and feelings without becoming them. It creates a space between you and your financial narratives, giving you the freedom to choose a different story, a different response.

This isn't about becoming a financial stoic, devoid of emotion. It's about being able to feel your emotions without being consumed by them. It's about acknowledging the fear, the shame, the anger, but not letting those emotions dictate your actions. It's about responding to your financial reality from a place of conscious awareness, rather than unconscious reaction. This takes practice. It takes courage. It takes a willingness to be uncomfortable. But the payoff is immense: genuine financial freedom, rooted in internal peace, not external circumstances.

Abundance as a State of Being

Ultimately, abundance isn't a number in your bank account. It's a state of being. It's the feeling of sufficiency. It's the knowledge that you have everything you need, in this moment, to take the next wise step. It's the capacity to create, to share, to contribute, from a place of overflow, not deficit. And you can only access that state of being when you are truly present. When you're grounded in the now, you're not chasing abundance; you're embodying it. It's a subtle but real shift. It's the difference between constantly striving and naturally attracting. Does that land?

The books like The Psychology of Money (paid link) and Mind Over Money (paid link) are invaluable for understanding the cognitive biases and behavioral patterns that affect our finances. But even those brilliant insights fall flat if you can't bring them into your present moment awareness. Knowledge without presence is just more data to intellectualize. It doesn't change your lived experience. True change happens when you integrate the intellectual understanding with the felt experience, and that integration requires presence.

So, stop running. Stop chasing. Stop pretending. Turn towards your money, not with fear, but with curiosity. Turn towards your discomfort, not with judgment, but with compassion. This is where the healing begins. This is where true, sustainable abundance is cultivated. Not in the future, but right here, right now.

FAQ

Q: Isn't focusing on the present irresponsible? Don't I need to plan for the future?

A: Absolutely not. Focusing on the present isn't about ignoring the future; it's about being grounded enough in the now to make wise, conscious decisions for the future. When you're truly present, your plans are more aligned, more intentional, and less driven by anxiety. You can't build a stable future from a place of internal chaos. Presence brings clarity and calm to your planning process.

Q: How can I be present with my money when I'm constantly stressed about not having enough?

A: This is precisely why presence is so crucial. The stress itself is a signal that you're operating from a place of fear, often rooted in past experiences or future projections. Presence allows you to acknowledge that stress without letting it consume you. It's about observing the feeling, rather than being the feeling. Start with small moments. Five minutes a day. Just observe. The goal isn't to eliminate the stress immediately, but to build your capacity to be with it, and from that space, choose a different response.

Q: What if I discover really uncomfortable truths about my money habits when I practice presence?

A: That's the point! The uncomfortable truths are the raw material for healing and growth. You can't change what you refuse to see. Presence doesn't make the discomfort disappear; it makes you capable of facing it. And when you face it, you can begin to understand its roots and consciously choose new patterns. This is where true change happens, not in avoidance.

Your challenge for today: For the next 24 hours, every time you think about money, pause. Take one deep breath. Just one. And notice what's happening in your body and your mind. Don't try to change it. Just notice. That's it. One breath. One moment of presence. Start there.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover the uncomfortable truth about presence and financial abundance. Learn how a lack of presence, not budgeting, may be hindering your wealth. Explore a ne
author: Kalesh
